summary:
Impressionism was an art movement that emerged in France in the 1860s and 1870s, characterized by loose brushwork, the direct study of natural light and its effects, and an interest in capturing momentary sensory impressions rather than carefully composed historical or mythological subjects. It represented a radical break with academic painting conventions and proved enormously influential on nearly all subsequent Western art.

The movement's name derived mockingly from Claude Monet's painting Impression, Sunrise (1872), which a hostile critic used as a term of dismissal. The Impressionists embraced the label. Their core group — Monet, Pierre-Auguste Renoir, Camille Pissarro, Edgar Degas, Alfred Sisley, Berthe Morisot, and Paul Cézanne — organized eight independent exhibitions between 1874 and 1886, bypassing the official Salon that controlled access to the French art world. Their subjects — Paris street scenes, cafés and dance halls, railway stations, gardens, water, and light — reflected the rapidly changing modern urban and suburban world they inhabited.

Technically, the Impressionists painted in short, visible brushstrokes of pure color applied directly from the tube, exploiting the discoveries of optical science that showed how the eye blended colors placed adjacent on a canvas. Their willingness to paint outdoors (en plein air) to capture the transient effects of light — the way a haystack looks different at different times of day, the shimmer of sunlight on water — represented a new priority of perception and feeling over idealized representation.

Although initially ridiculed by critics and the public, Impressionism gradually won acceptance and became enormously popular. Its influence on subsequent art was profound: Post-Impressionism (Cézanne, Van Gogh, Gauguin, Seurat) developed its techniques in new directions that led toward the abstract art of the twentieth century. Impressionism remains among the most beloved and economically valued art styles in history.
